title: C Enum
content:
### Enum in C

An enumeration (`enum`) is a user-defined type consisting of a set of named integer constants.

### Definition

```c
enum Color {RED, GREEN, BLUE};
```

### Values

By default, the first enumerator has value 0, and each subsequent value increments by 1.

```c
enum Color {RED, GREEN, BLUE};
// RED = 0, GREEN = 1, BLUE = 2
```

### Custom Values

```c
enum Status {OK = 200, NOT_FOUND = 404, ERROR = 500};
```

### Benefits of Enums

| Benefit | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| Readability | `Color c = RED` vs `int c = 0` |
| Type safety | Compiler can warn about type mismatches |
| Maintainability | Adding values is easy |
| Debugging | Symbolic names show in debugger |

### Enum vs #define Constants

| Feature | enum | #define |
|---------|------|---------|
| Type checking | Yes (enumerated type) | No (text substitution) |
| Scope | Block-scoped | Global (from point of definition) |
| Debugger support | Symbolic names visible | Names lost in compilation |
| Values | Auto-incrementing | Must be assigned manually |

---

> **Key Point:** Enums improve code readability and maintainability. Use them instead of magic numbers or multiple #define constants.

quiz.question: What value does the first enum member default to?
